summary: Gruber observes that Twitter's reliability problems have shifted from complete outages to something more insidious: silently dropping individual tweets. He documents the issue with @replies not being delivered, corroborated by multiple users and developer Craig Hockenberry. Rather than the familiar all-or-nothing downtime, Twitter now appears up while quietly losing messages. Gruber argues this new failure mode is psychologically worse because users can't tell when their messages have been lost.
key_points: Twitter's reliability issues shifted from obvious total outages to silent, partial message loss
The @reply system specifically was dropping tweets without any notification to sender or recipient
Multiple users and developer Craig Hockenberry independently confirmed and documented the problem
A known, visible failure is preferable to an invisible, indeterminate one because users can at least adapt their behavior
Making reliability indeterminate undermines trust more than simple downtime does
money_quotes: Psychologically, I find it far better to have a big problem (the entire service being down) that everyone knows about than to have a smaller problem (occasional tweets silently disappearing into the ether) that, because of the very nature of the problem, you don't even know about.
In short, making Twitter's reliability indeterminate is worse, not better.
The old way, you at least knew instantly what the deal was. Twitter was up, or Twitter was down.
Now, though, you can't count on anything.
If it was up, you could pretty much count on your tweets working.
key_insight: A system that fails visibly and completely is more trustworthy than one that appears to work while silently losing data, because indeterminate reliability is worse than known downtime.
